original_hypothesis.title: No Withdrawal Request Cancellation Creates Permanent Fund Lock Risk
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Users who call requestWithdrawal() transfer their LRON shares to the vault contract. There is no mechanism to cancel a withdrawal request and reclaim shares. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() is never called for the current epoch (due to operator unavailability, the onlyOperator bug INV-ACCESS-001, or contract pause), user shares are permanently locked in the vault with no recovery path. Combined with the onlyOperator bug, if the owner is also flagged as operator, ALL withdrawal requests become permanently irrecoverable.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: requestWithdrawal transfers shares via _transfer(msg.sender, address(this), _shares). The only path to recover value is through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch (burns shares, sends WRON to escrow) followed by redeem(epoch). There is no cancelWithdrawal function to return shares to the user.

proof_of_signal.pattern: shares transferred to vault via _transfer with no cancel/recovery mechanism; finalization is sole exit path

preconditions: User calls requestWithdrawal with non-zero shares
finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch is not called for the current epoch

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: One-way transfer with no cancellation mechanism
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User loses control of shares with only operator-dependent recovery
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cancelWithdrawal function; no timeout-based auto-return

analysis_questions: Is there any alternate path for the vault to return locked shares?
Could the owner use a different function to process locked shares?
What is the maximum duration funds could be locked?
validation_requirements: Confirm no alternate recovery path exists in the codebase
Test scenario: requestWithdrawal followed by indefinite finalization delay

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users request withdrawal during normal operation. The operator (or owner) becomes unavailable or the onlyOperator bug prevents finalization. All shares in the current epoch's locked pool are permanently inaccessible. Users cannot withdraw, redeem, or transfer those shares.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all shares submitted via requestWithdrawal for the unfinalized epoch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who called requestWithdrawal for the affected epoch

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for withdrawal cancellation or recovery from failed finalization

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Locked shares visible on-chain in vault balance

validation_result.impact_summary: Users who call requestWithdrawal() irrevocably transfer LRON shares to the vault with no cancellation path. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() cannot be executed, those shares are permanently locked.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that requestWithdrawal() transfers shares from users to the vault contract via _transfer(msg.sender, address(this), _shares), and the ONLY recovery path is through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() followed by redeem(epoch). There is no cancelWithdrawal function, no emergency recovery, and no timeout-based auto-return. A thorough search of all functions in LiquidRon.sol confirms no alternate path exists to return locked shares to users.

Finalization depends on multiple preconditions: (1) an authorized caller must exist (owner who is NOT flagged as operator, per the buggy onlyOperator modifier), (2) the contract must not be paused, and (3) the vault must hold sufficient WRON to cover the locked shares' asset value. If most funds are delegated to validators, finalization will revert because SafeERC20.safeTransfer will fail due to insufficient WRON balance. This requires a prior undelegation step, adding another dependency.

The onlyOperator modifier bug (if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert) means only the owner (when NOT also an operator) can call finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. If the owner calls updateOperator(owner, true), even the owner gets locked out of all operator functions, making finalization impossible and all pending withdrawal requests permanently irrecoverable.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 282-292 of LiquidRon.sol confirm the one-way share transfer with no cancellation. Lines 249-260 confirm finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch is the sole exit path. The onlyOperator modifier at line 88 confirms the access control bug. No cancelWithdrawal or emergency recovery function exists anywhere in the codebase.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Shares are irrevocably transferred from user to vault contract. No cancel function exists to reverse this. If finalization never occurs, shares are permanently locked.

This is the ONLY path to process locked shares. It requires onlyOperator access (buggy), whenNotPaused, and sufficient WRON balance in vault. Failure of any precondition blocks all pending withdrawal requests with no alternative.

Inverted logic: designated operators always revert. Only owner who is NOT flagged as operator can pass. If updateOperator(owner, true) is called, even the owner is locked out, making finalization impossible.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls requestWithdrawal(_shares) - shares transferred to vault via _transfer(msg.sender, address(this), _shares) at line 289
3. To recover value, finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() must be called (line 249) - requires onlyOperator + whenNotPaused + sufficient WRON
4. After finalization, user calls redeem(epoch) (line 297) to claim assets from escrow
5. If step 3 never completes, shares remain at address(this) permanently - no cancel or recovery path exists
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: requestWithdrawal (line 282) transfers shares to vault via _transfer at line 289. The only recovery is finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch (line 249) which burns shares via _withdraw (line 256) and sends assets to escrow, followed by user calling redeem(epoch) (line 297). If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch cannot execute due to: (a) onlyOperator bug preventing access (line 88-91), (b) contract being paused, (c) insufficient WRON in vault (most delegated to validators), then locked shares are permanently inaccessible. No cancelWithdrawal, no emergency withdraw, no timeout recovery exists in the entire codebase.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script (LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol) shows the deployer calling delegateAmount directly as owner (not setting up operators), confirming only the owner can call operator functions. The script does not set up any operator addresses, meaning finalization depends solely on the owner.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1 (operator unavailability): Users request withdrawal. Owner/operator becomes unavailable (key loss, compromise, goes offline). No finalization occurs. Shares locked permanently. Scenario 2 (onlyOperator self-lock): Owner calls updateOperator(owner, true). Now onlyOperator modifier reverts for everyone including owner (line 88: msg.sender != owner() evaluates false, but operator[msg.sender] evaluates true, so false || true = true → revert). All operator functions including finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch become inaccessible. All pending and future withdrawal requests are irrecoverable. Scenario 3 (insufficient liquidity): Most vault WRON is delegated to validators. finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch calls _withdraw which calls SafeERC20.safeTransfer. If vault WRON < required assets, transfer reverts. Operator must first undelegate, which itself requires onlyOperator access and may have protocol-level cooldown periods.
validation_result.impact_assessment: All users who call requestWithdrawal for an unfinalized epoch permanently lose access to their shares. The shares sit in the vault contract with no mechanism to return them. This is not just a theoretical concern - the onlyOperator bug means a single accidental updateOperator(owner, true) call permanently locks all future withdrawal requests. Even without the bug, the single-path dependency on operator availability and sufficient vault liquidity creates unacceptable fund lock risk.
recommended_mitigations: Add a cancelWithdrawal(uint256 _epoch) function that allows users to reclaim shares from unfinalised epochs: transfer shares back from vault to user and decrement lockedSharesPerEpoch
Add a timeout-based auto-cancellation: if an epoch is not finalized within N blocks, allow users to cancel their requests
Fix the onlyOperator modifier to: if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator();
Add an emergency recovery function (owner-only) that can return locked shares to users if finalization is blocked
Consider allowing partial finalization or multi-step finalization to handle liquidity constraints
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidRon with test parameters. 2. User deposits and receives LRON shares. 3. User calls requestWithdrawal(shares). 4. Verify shares transferred to vault. 5. Do NOT call finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. 6. Attempt to find any way to recover shares - confirm none exists. 7. For the onlyOperator lock scenario: call updateOperator(owner, true), then try calling finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch - confirm it reverts.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team considers operator dependency an acceptable trust assumption rather than a code defect
Whether an upgrade or migration mechanism exists outside the analyzed codebase that could rescue locked funds
additional_testing_needed: Test requestWithdrawal followed by indefinite delay without finalization - confirm no recovery path
Test updateOperator(owner, true) followed by attempting any operator function - confirm permanent lockout
Test finalization when vault WRON balance is insufficient due to delegation - confirm revert behavior
Test the full withdrawal flow under various liquidity conditions
